skill.name: azure-quotas
skill.description: Guide Azure quota checks, usage reviews, regional capacity comparisons, and quota increase requests with Azure CLI workflows.
skill.summary: Check Azure quota limits, current usage, and regional capacity before deployment.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, network, and high-risk keyword patterns. Manual review found no malware, credential access, exfiltration, or prompt injection, but the skill does guide Azure CLI usage and includes quota mutation commands that require operator caution.

medium_findings.title: Azure CLI Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The static external command findings are mostly false positives because the files document Azure CLI examples, not executable scripts. They still represent a real operational risk because the skill instructs users or agents to run az quota commands against Azure subscriptions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines clearly show Azure CLI command guidance. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the commands are documented examples with placeholders.

medium_findings.title: Quota Mutation Commands Documented
medium_findings.description: The skill includes az quota update and az quota create examples. These commands can change requested quota limits in a real Azure subscription if run with sufficient permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and their purpose is quota changes. The examples use placeholders, so this is not evidence of malicious account modification.

low_findings.title: Official Azure Documentation and Portal Links
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded links to Microsoft Learn and the Azure Portal. No evidence found of hidden external endpoints, telemetry, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to Azure Portal or Microsoft Learn documentation and are used as references. I found no code that sends user data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Pattern Matches Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and PowerShell findings do not indicate dangerous behavior in context. The cited lines are metadata, command tables, ordinary text, or references to Azure CLI output fields.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited locations showed no weak cryptographic primitive and no executable PowerShell invocation. The findings appear to be keyword collisions in Markdown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The static external command findings are mostly false positives because the files document Azure CLI examples, not executable scripts. They still represent a real operational risk because the skill instructs users or agents to run az quota commands against Azure subscriptions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines clearly show Azure CLI command guidance. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the commands are documented examples with placeholders.

dangerous_patterns.title: Quota Mutation Commands Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes az quota update and az quota create examples. These commands can change requested quota limits in a real Azure subscription if run with sufficient permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and their purpose is quota changes. The examples use placeholders, so this is not evidence of malicious account modification.

content.value_statement: Azure deployments can fail when subscriptions lack regional quota or use the wrong quota resource name. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Azure CLI checks, usage reviews, and quota increase planning.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install and use the Azure CLI quota extension.
Shows how to list quota limits for Azure resource providers and regions.
Guides current usage checks with az quota usage commands.
Helps compare available capacity across candidate Azure regions.
Documents quota increase workflows and request status checks.
Clarifies when to fall back to Microsoft Learn service limit documentation.
limitations: Requires Azure CLI access and an authenticated Azure subscription.
Does not automatically verify tenant permissions or subscription policy.
Some Azure providers do not support the quota API.
Quota increase approvals depend on Azure service rules and capacity.

use_cases.title: Pre-deployment Capacity Check
use_cases.description: Confirm current quota, usage, and remaining capacity before deploying virtual machines, networks, or managed services.

use_cases.title: Region Selection Review
use_cases.description: Compare quota availability across regions before choosing a deployment location for production workloads.

use_cases.title: Quota Increase Preparation
use_cases.description: Prepare quota increase requests and track status when existing subscription limits are too low.

prompt_templates.title: Check One Quota
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me check the Azure quota and current usage for one resource in one region. Ask for the subscription, provider, region, and resource family first.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Deployment Capacity
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate whether my planned Azure deployment fits within current quota. Compare required capacity with current usage and quota limits.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Candidate Regions
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a region comparison plan for Azure quota availability. Include the az quota commands and a table of capacity to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Quota Increase Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare an Azure quota increase request. Identify the quota resource name, needed limit, command sequence, permissions, and status checks.

output_examples.input: Can I deploy 80 DSv3 vCPUs in East US?
output: A capacity check plan with quota discovery, current usage lookup, and available vCPU calculation.
A clear pass or fail result after the user supplies command output.
Next steps for quota increase or alternate region selection if capacity is insufficient.

output_examples.input: Which region should I use for this workload?
output: A region comparison workflow covering each candidate region.
A table format for limit, usage, available capacity, and notes.
A recommendation based on quota headroom and provider support.
A troubleshooting path to identify the provider, scope, quota resource name, and current usage.
Guidance on requesting an increase or selecting a region with available capacity.

best_practices: Run read-only quota list and usage commands before any quota update command.
Use exact quota resource names discovered from az quota list output.
Keep a capacity buffer above the immediate deployment requirement.
anti_patterns: Do not assume REST API or portal values marked no limit mean unlimited capacity.
Do not guess quota resource names from ARM resource types.
Do not request quota changes without confirming subscription, region, and business need.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. Users or agents still need authenticated Azure CLI access to run commands.

faq.question: What permissions are needed?
faq.answer: Reader permissions are enough for viewing many quotas. Quota changes require permissions such as Quota Request Operator.

faq.question: Can it request quota increases?
faq.answer: It documents the workflow and command pattern. Approval and final changes depend on Azure permissions and service policy.

faq.question: Why does it prefer Azure CLI?
faq.answer: The skill states that Azure CLI gives clearer quota results and better errors for unsupported providers.

faq.question: Does it support every Azure service?
faq.answer: No. Some providers do not support the quota API, so official service limit documentation may be required.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
